title: Let and Const (ES6)
content:
### Let and Const

ES6 introduced `let` and `const` as **block-scoped** alternatives to `var`.

---

### `let` -- Mutable, Block-Scoped

```js
let x = 10;
x = 20; // OK: can reassign

if (true) {
  let y = 30;
}
console.log(y); // Error: y is not defined (block-scoped)
```

### `const` -- Immutable Binding

```js
const PI = 3.14;
PI = 3; // Error: cannot reassign a constant

const person = { name: "John" };
person.name = "Jane"; // OK: object properties CAN change
```

> `const` means **the binding is constant**, not the value. Object properties and array elements can still change.

---

### `var` vs `let` vs `const`

| Feature | `var` | `let` | `const` |
|---------|-------|-------|--------|
| **Scope** | Function | Block | Block |
| **Reassign** | Yes | Yes | No |
| **Redeclare** | Yes | No | No |
| **Hoisted** | Yes (`undefined`) | Yes (TDZ) | Yes (TDZ) |
| **Use** | Avoid | When reassigning | Default choice |

---

### Temporal Dead Zone (TDZ)

`let` and `const` are hoisted but cannot be accessed before declaration:

```js
console.log(x); // ReferenceError: Cannot access before initialization
let x = 5;
```

This zone between hoisting and declaration is the **Temporal Dead Zone**.

---

> **Modern Standard:** Use `const` by default. Use `let` only when you need to reassign. Never use `var` in new code.

quiz.question: Which keyword creates a variable that cannot be reassigned?
